documents.title: Base Building Fundamentals
documents.content:
Base building is the foundation of all running success. It develops aerobic capacity, strengthens connective tissues, and prepares your body for harder training.

During base building:
- 80% of runs should be at an easy, conversational pace
- Focus on gradually increasing weekly mileage
- Run consistently (4-6 days per week) rather than sporadically
- Build for 4-8 weeks before adding intensity

Signs your base is solid:
- You can hold a conversation during easy runs
- Your heart rate stays relatively low at the same pace
- You recover quickly between runs
- You feel strong at the end of long runs

Common mistakes:
- Running easy days too fast (this undermines recovery and aerobic development)
- Adding speed work before aerobic base is established
- Increasing mileage too quickly (stick to 10% rule)
- Not being patient—base building takes months, not weeks

documents.title: The Purpose of Easy Runs
documents.content:
Easy runs are the backbone of any training program, typically making up 80% of weekly mileage. They serve multiple purposes that hard running cannot accomplish.

Physiological benefits:
- Builds capillary density for better oxygen delivery
- Increases mitochondria (cellular powerhouses)
- Improves fat-burning efficiency
- Strengthens tendons and ligaments safely
- Develops running economy through repetition

How slow is easy?
- You should be able to hold a full conversation
- Heart rate typically 65-75% of max
- If you're breathing hard, you're going too fast
- Pace may vary significantly based on conditions (heat, fatigue, terrain)

Why runners run easy days too fast:
- Ego—easy pace 'feels' too slow
- Strava/social comparison pressure
- Misunderstanding of how fitness is built
- Confusing effort with benefit

The 80/20 principle: Elite runners spend 80% of training at low intensity. This polarized approach produces the best results because hard days can be truly hard when easy days are truly easy.

documents.title: Tempo Runs Explained
documents.content:
Tempo runs train your body to clear lactate more efficiently, raising your lactate threshold—the pace you can sustain for extended periods.

What is tempo pace?
- Often described as 'comfortably hard'
- Roughly the pace you could race for one hour
- You can speak in short phrases but not full sentences
- Heart rate around 85-90% of max

Types of tempo workouts:
1. Classic tempo: 20-40 minutes continuous at tempo pace
2. Cruise intervals: Shorter segments (e.g., 3x10 min) with brief recovery
3. Tempo progression: Start easier, finish at tempo pace

When to include tempo runs:
- After building a solid aerobic base
- Once per week for most runners
- 8-12 weeks before a goal race

Common tempo mistakes:
- Starting too fast (tempo should feel sustainable)
- Running tempos when fatigued from other hard sessions
- Doing tempo runs too frequently
- Racing every tempo run instead of training controlled

Tempo runs are about training your physiology, not proving fitness. Run them controlled.

documents.title: Interval Training for Runners
documents.content:
Intervals improve VO2max, running economy, and speed. They're high-intensity efforts with recovery periods.

Common interval formats:
- Short intervals: 200m-400m at faster than 5K pace, full recovery
- Medium intervals: 800m-1000m at 5K pace, 2-3 min recovery
- Long intervals: 1200m-mile at 5K-10K pace, 3-4 min recovery

Purpose by type:
- Short (200-400m): Neuromuscular speed, form, leg turnover
- Medium (800m): VO2max development, 5K specific
- Long (1200m+): Race pace confidence, sustained effort

Recovery between intervals:
- Should be long enough to perform the next rep at the same quality
- Walking or slow jogging, not standing still
- Typically 50-100% of the work interval duration

Progression:
- Start with fewer reps, build volume over weeks
- Aim for consistency across reps, not starting fast and fading
- If final reps are much slower, the workout was too hard

Typical frequency: Once per week during a training cycle. Two speed sessions (tempo + intervals) per week maximum for most runners.

documents.title: Long Run Principles
documents.content:
The long run builds endurance, mental toughness, and teaches your body to burn fat efficiently. It's the cornerstone of distance training.

Long run guidelines:
- Typically 20-30% of weekly mileage
- Pace should be conversational (slower than you think)
- Build duration gradually: add 10-15 minutes or 1-2 miles per week
- Every 3-4 weeks, cut back for recovery

Pace considerations:
- Most long runs should be 60-90 seconds per mile slower than goal race pace
- Running long runs too fast compromises recovery without additional benefit
- Exception: Race-specific long runs with tempo or marathon pace segments

Progressive long runs:
- Start easy, finish at a moderate effort
- Teaches pacing and builds confidence
- Example: 16 miles with last 4 at marathon pace

Fueling practice:
- Long runs are when you practice race-day nutrition
- Test gels, drinks, and timing
- Simulate race morning routine

Mental benefits:
- Builds confidence for race distance
- Teaches you to run through discomfort
- Develops patience and pacing discipline

documents.title: Understanding Tapering
documents.content:
Tapering is the strategic reduction of training volume before a goal race. It allows your body to fully recover and absorb fitness while maintaining sharpness.

Typical taper duration:
- 5K: 7-10 days
- 10K: 10-14 days
- Half marathon: 2 weeks
- Marathon: 3 weeks

How to taper:
- Reduce volume by 40-60% overall
- Maintain some intensity (shorter, sharp workouts)
- Keep frequency similar (don't suddenly stop running)
- Prioritize sleep and nutrition

Taper feelings (normal):
- Phantom pains and twinges (you notice things you'd ignore while training)
- Restless energy (your body is storing up)
- Doubt ('Have I done enough?')
- Legs feel heavy initially, then fresh

Taper mistakes:
- Panicking and cramming in extra workouts
- Testing fitness with hard efforts close to race day
- Dramatically changing diet or sleep patterns
- Overthinking everything

Trust the taper. The fitness is already in your legs from months of training. The goal now is freshness, not more fitness.

documents.title: Heart Rate Training Basics
documents.content:
Heart rate training provides objective feedback about effort, independent of pace. It accounts for fatigue, heat, stress, and illness.

Heart rate zones (approximate):
- Zone 1 (50-60% max): Recovery, warm-up
- Zone 2 (60-70% max): Easy runs, base building (this is where most running should be)
- Zone 3 (70-80% max): Moderate, tempo runs
- Zone 4 (80-90% max): Threshold, hard intervals
- Zone 5 (90-100% max): VO2max, short sprints

Benefits of heart rate training:
- Prevents running easy days too fast
- Accounts for external factors (heat increases HR at same pace)
- Shows aerobic fitness improvements (lower HR at same pace)
- Helps recognize overtraining (elevated resting HR)

Limitations:
- Cardiac drift: HR rises during long runs even at same effort
- Heart rate lag: Takes time to respond to effort changes
- Individual variation: Max HR formulas are estimates

Getting started:
- Use perceived effort alongside HR (learn what different zones feel like)
- Don't obsess over exact numbers
- Note trends over time rather than single data points

documents.title: Active Recovery Strategies
documents.content:
Active recovery promotes blood flow and healing without adding training stress. It's the middle ground between complete rest and regular training.

Active recovery methods:
- Very easy jogging (recovery run pace)
- Walking
- Swimming or pool running
- Easy cycling
- Gentle yoga or stretching

When to use active recovery:
- Day after a hard workout or race
- During deload weeks
- When feeling minor fatigue (not injury)
- Between races in a multi-race weekend

Recovery run guidelines:
- Truly easy pace (embarrassingly slow)
- 20-40 minutes maximum
- If it doesn't feel like recovery, you're going too hard
- Skip if you need complete rest

Other recovery practices:
- Foam rolling (10-15 minutes targeting tight areas)
- Compression (socks or sleeves after hard efforts)
- Massage (professional or self-massage)
- Contrast baths (alternating hot and cold)
- Sleep (the most powerful recovery tool)

documents.title: Deload Weeks
documents.content:
Deload weeks are planned reductions in training volume that allow accumulated fatigue to dissipate while maintaining fitness.

Typical deload structure:
- Every 3-4 weeks during a training cycle
- Reduce volume by 30-50%
- Maintain some intensity but shorter/fewer
- Focus on quality over quantity

Why deload:
- Training stress accumulates over weeks
- Adaptation happens during recovery, not during hard training
- Prevents overtraining syndrome
- Keeps motivation and freshness high

Signs you need a deload:
- Persistent fatigue despite sleep
- Declining performance in workouts
- Elevated resting heart rate
- Irritability or mood changes
- Increased susceptibility to illness
- Loss of motivation

Deload week example (30 miles/week runner):
- Normal week: 30 miles with tempo + intervals
- Deload week: 18-20 miles with one light tempo

Common mistake: Viewing deloads as lost training. In reality, deloads are where fitness solidifies. Trust the process.

documents.title: Post-Run Recovery Routine
documents.content:
What you do immediately after running significantly impacts recovery and your ability to train consistently.

First 30 minutes (the golden window):
- Light movement (walking, not sitting immediately)
- Hydration (16-24 oz fluid)
- Protein + carbs (chocolate milk, smoothie, or snack)
- Change out of sweaty clothes

Post-run nutrition:
- Aim for 15-25g protein within 30-60 minutes
- Include carbohydrates to replenish glycogen
- Real food is fine—doesn't need to be a special shake
- Larger meals can wait 1-2 hours

Stretching and mobility:
- Dynamic movement beats static stretching post-run
- Focus on hips, hamstrings, calves, quads
- 5-10 minutes is sufficient
- Foam rolling for tight spots

Later in the day:
- Stay lightly active (walking, standing)
- Elevate legs if heavy or swollen
- Compression garments if desired
- Prioritize sleep that night

Mental recovery:
- Log your run (captures data and reflection)
- Note how you felt (builds body awareness)
- Celebrate completing the workout

documents.title: Fueling for Training
documents.content:
Proper nutrition supports training adaptation, recovery, and performance. Underfueling undermines your training.

Daily nutrition principles:
- Adequate calories to support training load
- Carbohydrates are your primary running fuel
- Protein for muscle repair (1.2-1.6g per kg body weight)
- Don't fear healthy fats

Pre-run fueling:
- Easy runs under 60 min: Can run fasted or light snack
- Hard workouts: Eat 2-3 hours before, or light snack 30-60 min before
- Avoid high fat/fiber close to running (GI distress)
- Individual tolerance varies—experiment in training

During run fueling:
- Runs under 60 min: Water usually sufficient
- 60-90 min: 30-60g carbs/hour for hard efforts
- 90+ min: 60-90g carbs/hour becomes important
- Practice in training what you'll do in races

Post-run:
- Protein + carbs within 30-60 minutes
- Larger balanced meal within 2 hours
- Hydrate to replace losses

General guidelines:
- Don't diet hard during heavy training
- Listen to hunger cues
- Consistency matters more than perfection

documents.title: Hydration for Runners
documents.content:
Proper hydration supports performance, recovery, and health. Both under-hydration and over-hydration can cause problems.

Daily hydration:
- General guideline: Half your body weight in ounces, plus more for exercise
- Urine color: Pale yellow indicates good hydration
- Thirst is a reasonable guide outside of exercise

Before running:
- Drink 16-20 oz water 2-3 hours before
- Another 8 oz 20-30 minutes before
- Don't chug large amounts right before

During running:
- Short runs (<60 min): Often don't need fluids unless hot
- Longer runs: Aim for 4-8 oz every 15-20 minutes
- Drink to thirst rather than forcing excessive fluid
- In heat, may need more frequent intake

Electrolytes:
- Become important for runs over 60-90 minutes
- Especially in heat or if you're a heavy/salty sweater
- Sports drinks, salt tabs, or electrolyte mixes
- Sodium is the primary electrolyte lost in sweat

Post-run:
- Replace approximately 16-24 oz per pound lost
- Include electrolytes after hard/long/hot runs
- Continue hydrating through the day

documents.title: Race Day Nutrition
documents.content:
Race day is not the time to try anything new. Your nutrition strategy should be practiced multiple times in training.

The night before:
- Familiar, carb-rich meal
- Avoid excessive fiber or new foods
- Moderate portion—not a massive pasta feast
- Hydrate well

Race morning:
- Eat 2-4 hours before start
- Familiar foods you've tested
- Examples: Toast with peanut butter, oatmeal, banana, bagel
- Light and carb-focused, easy to digest

30-60 minutes before:
- Small carb snack if desired (banana, gel, sports drink)
- Sip fluids, don't chug

During the race:
- 5K/10K: Usually no fueling needed
- Half marathon: Consider gels at miles 5-6 and 9-10
- Marathon: Start fueling early (mile 4-5), every 4-5 miles

Gel/fueling tips:
- Take with water to aid absorption
- Don't wait until you feel depleted
- Steady intake beats sporadic large amounts
- Practice exact products and timing in long runs

Post-race:
- Hydrate immediately
- Eat when appetite returns
- Protein + carbs for recovery
- Celebrate!

documents.title: Common Running Injuries
documents.content:
Understanding common injuries helps with prevention and early recognition. Most running injuries are overuse injuries that develop gradually.

Runner's knee (patellofemoral pain):
- Pain around or behind kneecap
- Often worse going downstairs or after sitting
- Usually caused by weakness in hips/glutes
- Treatment: Strength training, reducing intensity

IT band syndrome:
- Pain on outside of knee
- Often appears at same point in runs
- Related to hip weakness and running mechanics
- Treatment: Hip strengthening, foam rolling, reduced mileage

Plantar fasciitis:
- Heel/arch pain, worst in morning
- Stabbing pain with first steps
- Related to calf tightness, arch support, overuse
- Treatment: Calf stretches, rolling on ball, supportive shoes

Shin splints (medial tibial stress syndrome):
- Pain along inner shin bone
- Common in new runners or rapid mileage increases
- Treatment: Rest, ice, gradual return, check shoes

Achilles tendinopathy:
- Pain in Achilles tendon above heel
- Stiffness in morning, improves then worsens
- Treatment: Eccentric heel drops, gradual loading

Prevention for all: Gradual progression, strength training, adequate rest, proper shoes.

documents.title: IT Band Syndrome
documents.content:
IT band syndrome (ITBS) is one of the most common running injuries, causing pain on the outside of the knee.

Symptoms:
- Pain on outer knee, usually during running
- Often appears at the same distance/time into a run
- May start mild and progressively worsen
- Usually no pain when walking initially

Causes:
- Hip weakness, especially glutes
- Increased mileage or intensity too quickly
- Running on cambered surfaces
- Poor running mechanics
- Inadequate recovery

Immediate treatment:
- Reduce running volume or stop temporarily
- Ice after activity
- Foam rolling can help (but don't roll directly on the IT band—roll quads, glutes, TFL)

Long-term solutions:
- Hip strengthening exercises (clamshells, side-lying leg raises, single-leg squats)
- Glute bridges and monster walks
- Address any biomechanical issues
- Gradual return to running

Prevention:
- Consistent hip/glute strength work
- Gradual mileage increases
- Vary running surfaces
- Don't ignore early warning signs

When to see a professional: If pain doesn't improve with rest and self-treatment within 2-3 weeks.

documents.title: Plantar Fasciitis
documents.content:
Plantar fasciitis is inflammation of the tissue on the bottom of the foot, causing heel or arch pain.

Symptoms:
- Sharp heel pain, especially with first steps in morning
- Pain after sitting then standing
- Improves after warming up, may return after long runs
- Tender spot near heel

Causes:
- Tight calves and Achilles
- Weak foot muscles
- Sudden increase in running
- Worn-out shoes or inadequate support
- Excessive time on feet

Immediate treatment:
- Roll foot on frozen water bottle (ice + massage)
- Calf stretches (straight and bent knee)
- Night splint to maintain stretch while sleeping
- Supportive shoes even at home (avoid barefoot)

Long-term solutions:
- Address calf tightness (stretching, foam rolling)
- Strengthen foot intrinsic muscles (towel scrunches)
- Gradually load the plantar fascia (heel raises)
- Consider orthotics if needed

Running modifications:
- May be able to run through mild cases if warming up helps
- Avoid hills and speedwork initially
- Run on softer surfaces
- Stop if pain alters gait

Recovery timeline: Often 3-6 months. Patience is essential.

documents.title: Returning to Running After Injury
documents.content:
Coming back from injury requires patience. Rushing return is the most common cause of re-injury.

When to return:
- Pain-free in daily activities
- Can walk briskly without pain
- Swelling has resolved
- Normal range of motion restored

Return-to-run progression:
- Start with walk/run intervals
- Example: Walk 4 min, jog 1 min, repeat 5-6x
- Increase run intervals gradually over 1-2 weeks
- Run every other day initially

Progression rules:
- No pain during or after running
- Slight muscle soreness is okay, joint/tendon pain is not
- If pain returns, back off immediately
- Don't increase distance and intensity at the same time

What to monitor:
- Pain level during run (should be 0-2/10)
- Pain the next morning (should be no worse)
- Any swelling or stiffness

Mental aspects:
- Returning can be frustrating—accept where you are
- Fitness returns faster than you expect
- Use the time to work on weaknesses (strength, mobility)
- Trust the process

Common mistake: Returning too fast because you feel okay. The tissue is still healing even when pain resolves. Patience prevents re-injury.

documents.title: Overtraining Syndrome
documents.content:
Overtraining syndrome (OTS) occurs when training stress exceeds recovery capacity over an extended period. It's different from normal fatigue.

Warning signs:
- Persistent fatigue that doesn't improve with rest
- Declining performance despite consistent training
- Elevated resting heart rate
- Increased susceptibility to illness
- Mood changes: irritability, anxiety, depression
- Sleep disturbances (insomnia or oversleeping)
- Loss of motivation and enjoyment
- Persistent muscle soreness

Risk factors:
- Rapid increases in training volume or intensity
- Inadequate sleep
- Poor nutrition/underfueling
- High life stress combined with training stress
- Insufficient rest days
- Racing too frequently

Recovery from OTS:
- Complete rest may be necessary (days to weeks)
- Address nutrition and sleep
- Reduce life stressors where possible
- Very gradual return to training
- May take weeks to months to fully recover

Prevention:
- Follow periodized training with regular deloads
- Prioritize sleep (7-9 hours)
- Eat enough to fuel training
- Take rest days seriously
- Listen to your body's warning signs early

documents.title: When to Take Extra Rest
documents.content:
Knowing when to rest is as important as knowing how to train. Sometimes the best workout is no workout.

Take an extra rest day if:
- You're getting sick or fighting off illness
- Resting heart rate is elevated (5+ bpm above normal)
- You've had poor sleep for consecutive nights
- Persistent muscle soreness that hasn't improved
- Nagging pain that worsens when running
- Extreme fatigue or motivation is gone
- High life stress or emotional strain

Signs it's okay to run through tiredness:
- General fatigue that improves after warming up
- Mild muscle soreness from previous workout
- Motivation returns once running
- Normal resting heart rate
- No sharp pains

Adjusting your run:
- Not every day needs to be skipped entirely
- Option: Easy run instead of workout
- Option: Shorter run
- Option: Walk breaks if needed

Mindset shift:
- Rest is part of training, not a failure
- One missed run doesn't derail fitness
- But running through warning signs can cause injury
- When in doubt, rest

Truth: Consistent, healthy running over months beats pushing through and getting injured.

documents.title: Pre-Race Nerves and Anxiety
documents.content:
Pre-race nerves are normal and can actually help performance. The goal isn't to eliminate anxiety but to manage it.

Why we get nervous:
- Racing matters to us (that's good!)
- Uncertainty about outcome
- Physical stress response preparing for effort

Reframing anxiety:
- Nervousness and excitement feel similar
- Tell yourself: 'I'm excited' instead of 'I'm nervous'
- Anxiety means you care about performance
- Those feelings will disappear once the gun goes off

Practical strategies:
- Have a pre-race routine you follow every time
- Focus on process goals, not outcome goals
- Visualize the race going well
- Arrive early to avoid rushing
- Warm up as you normally would

Race morning tips:
- Stick to familiar foods and routine
- Avoid excessive caffeine if anxiety-prone
- Listen to music that calms or motivates you
- Move around rather than sitting and overthinking

During the race:
- Focus on the current mile, not the finish
- Use mantras when anxiety spikes
- Trust your training—you've done the work
- Breathe, relax your shoulders and hands

Post-race: Whether good or bad, reflect and learn. Every race teaches something.

documents.title: Dealing with a Bad Race
documents.content:
Bad races happen to everyone. How you respond determines whether it becomes a setback or a learning experience.

First, some perspective:
- One race doesn't define you as a runner
- Elite runners have bad races too
- Factors outside your control can affect performance

Common causes of bad races:
- Going out too fast
- Inadequate fueling or hydration
- Weather conditions
- Undertrained or overtrained
- Illness or injury you weren't aware of
- Life stress affecting performance
- Just a bad day (it happens)

Healthy response:
- Allow yourself to feel disappointed (it's natural)
- Give it a day or two before analyzing
- Look for objective lessons, not self-criticism
- Identify what you'd do differently
- Remember your good races and why you love running

Moving forward:
- Don't make reactive changes to training
- Don't immediately sign up for a redemption race
- Take a few easy days
- Return to training when mentally ready
- Focus on the next goal, not the past failure

The best runners have short memories for bad races and long memories for what they learned from them.

documents.title: Staying Motivated Through Training
documents.content:
Motivation fluctuates. The runners who succeed long-term have strategies for when motivation is low.

Building sustainable motivation:
- Set meaningful goals that excite you
- Focus on process (daily habits) not just outcomes
- Track progress to see improvement
- Find running partners or community
- Vary your routes and training

When motivation is low:
- Show up for an easy, short run—no pressure
- Remember why you started running
- Look back at your progress
- Sign up for a race to create accountability
- Try something different (trail, new route, running group)

Don't rely on motivation alone:
- Build running into your routine (habit over motivation)
- Lay out clothes the night before
- Schedule runs like appointments
- Make it easy to start (lower friction)
- Commit to just 10 minutes—often you'll continue

Natural low motivation periods:
- Right after a goal race (normal—take a mental break)
- Winter/dark mornings (use extra strategies)
- High life stress periods (reduce training load)

Motivation returns:
- Usually after a breakthrough run or race
- When you feel fitness building
- With the right running partners
- After forced rest (injury or illness)

Consistency beats occasional bursts of motivation.

documents.title: Running in the Heat
documents.content:
Heat significantly affects running performance. Adapting your training and expectations is essential for safety and progress.

How heat affects running:
- Heart rate increases at the same pace
- Perceived effort is higher
- Pace naturally slows
- Increased fluid and electrolyte losses
- Core temperature rises faster

Adjusting your training:
- Slow down—expect 30-60+ seconds per mile slower
- Use heart rate over pace as guide
- Run early morning or late evening
- Seek shaded routes
- Shorten workouts if necessary

Hydration in heat:
- Pre-hydrate in the hours before
- Carry fluids on runs over 45 minutes
- Include electrolytes for longer runs
- Weigh yourself before/after to track losses

Warning signs (stop immediately):
- Dizziness or confusion
- Nausea or vomiting
- Cessation of sweating
- Excessive fatigue
- Chills despite heat

Heat adaptation:
- Occurs over 10-14 days of heat exposure
- Run consistently in heat (safely) to adapt
- Fitness in heat transfers to better cool-weather performance

Race considerations: Check the forecast and adjust goal pace accordingly. Accept that heat will affect times.

documents.title: Hill Running
documents.content:
Hills build strength, power, and mental toughness. They're one of the most valuable training tools available.

Benefits of hill running:
- Strengthens glutes, hamstrings, calves
- Improves running economy
- Builds power without track speedwork
- Low impact compared to flat sprints
- Mental toughness from effort

Uphill running tips:
- Shorten stride and increase cadence
- Lean slightly forward from ankles, not waist
- Pump arms more actively
- Stay relaxed (shoulders down, hands loose)
- Maintain effort, not pace

Downhill running tips:
- Let gravity help you, don't brake excessively
- Quick, light steps
- Lean slightly forward, not back
- Use your arms for balance
- Relax and let your legs turnover

Hill workout examples:
- Hill repeats: 30-90 second efforts, jog down, repeat 6-10x
- Long hill: Sustained 4-8 minute climb at tempo effort
- Rolling route: Include hills naturally in a regular run

Common mistakes:
- Attacking hills too hard early in a race
- Tensing up and wasting energy
- Stopping at the top of a hill (push over the crest)

Philosophy: Run the hills when they come. They're making you stronger.

documents.title: Strength Training for Runners
documents.content:
Strength training improves running economy, prevents injuries, and helps maintain speed as you fatigue. Most runners don't do enough.

Key benefits:
- Injury prevention (stronger muscles and connective tissue)
- Better running economy (less energy wasted)
- Maintained form when tired
- Improved power for hills and finishing kicks

Essential exercises:
- Single-leg work: Lunges, step-ups, single-leg deadlifts, Bulgarian split squats
- Hip strength: Clamshells, side-lying leg raises, hip bridges, monster walks
- Core: Planks, dead bugs, bird dogs, pallof press
- Glutes: Hip thrusts, glute bridges, squats

Frequency and timing:
- 2-3x per week is ideal
- 20-30 minutes is sufficient
- Separate from hard running days if possible
- Or do light strength after easy runs

Progression:
- Start with bodyweight
- Progress to bands, then weights
- Focus on control and form over heavy weight
- Build over weeks and months

Common excuses:
- 'I'll get bulky' (you won't—runners don't eat or lift for bulk)
- 'Running is enough' (it's not—muscle imbalances develop)
- 'No time' (20 minutes 2x/week makes a difference)

Prioritize hip and glute strength—this is where most runners are weak.

documents.title: Cross-Training Options
documents.content:
Cross-training maintains fitness while reducing running impact. It's valuable during injury recovery, high mileage weeks, or for variety.

Best cross-training for runners:

Pool running (aqua jogging):
- Mimics running motion without impact
- Excellent for injury recovery
- Can do workouts (intervals, tempo)
- Boring but effective

Cycling:
- Builds leg strength and endurance
- Low impact on joints
- Good for recovery days
- Longer duration needed (2-3x running time)

Swimming:
- Full body, zero impact
- Good for recovery
- Builds upper body (useful for arm swing)
- Different enough to feel like mental break

Elliptical:
- Similar motion to running
- Low impact
- Can maintain running fitness effectively
- Can do running workouts (intervals, tempo)

Cross-country skiing / rowing:
- Full body cardio
- Excellent aerobic development
- Builds overall strength

When to cross-train:
- Recovery days (replace easy run)
- Injury (maintain fitness while healing)
- High mileage weeks (reduce impact load)
- Mental freshness (break from running)

Note: Cross-training complements running but doesn't replace running-specific fitness. When healthy, prioritize running.

documents.title: Canova Periodization Principles
documents.content:
Renato Canova's periodization is built around natural progression and race-specific preparation.

Core Principles:
- Training should progress from general to specific
- Volume before intensity in each phase
- Quality sessions require full recovery
- Rest days are training days

The Four Phases:
1. TRANSITION (12+ weeks out): Rebuild after previous cycle, easy running, strides
2. GENERAL CONDITIONING (8-12 weeks out): Build aerobic base, introduce tempos
3. FUNDAMENTAL (4-8 weeks out): Event-specific endurance, longer intervals
4. SPECIFIC (0-4 weeks out): Race-pace work, sharpening, taper

Key Canova Concepts:
- 'Special blocks' - clusters of race-specific workouts
- 'Natural running' - relaxed form at pace
- Progressive overload within workouts
- Descending intervals (longer to shorter, faster)
- Recovery matters as much as training

documents.title: Canova Signature Workouts
documents.content:
Canova's signature workouts develop race-specific fitness through progressive and descending structures.

Progressive Tempo:
- Start at easy pace, progress to tempo, finish at threshold
- Teaches body to run fast when tired
- Example: 12K as 4K easy -> 4K moderate -> 4K tempo

Descending Ladder:
- Longer intervals first, shorter intervals last
- Each interval faster as distance decreases
- Example: 3K, 2K, 1K, 1K with decreasing rest

Race Pace Repeats:
- Extended efforts at goal race pace
- Builds confidence and pacing skill
- Example: 6 x 1 mile at marathon pace with 90s rest

Long Run with Tempo Finish:
- Long slow distance followed by uptempo finish
- Simulates racing on tired legs
- Example: 20 miles with final 5 at marathon pace

Special Blocks:
- Multiple quality sessions in condensed timeframe
- Forces adaptation under fatigue
- Used sparingly in specific phase

documents.title: Canova Pace Zones
documents.content:
Canova uses pace zones relative to goal race pace (RP) rather than heart rate zones.

Marathon Pace Zones:
- Easy: 25-30% slower than RP (~75% RP pace)
- Aerobic: 15-20% slower than RP (~82% RP pace)
- Medium: 10% slower than RP (~90% RP pace)
- Threshold: 5% faster than RP (~105% RP pace)
- Fast: 10%+ faster than RP

For Shorter Races (5K/10K):
- Same concept but RP is faster
- Longer intervals at 95-100% RP
- Shorter intervals at 102-105% RP
- Recovery runs significantly slower

How to Calculate:
- If marathon goal is 3:00 (6:52/mi), RP = 6:52/mi
- Easy pace: 8:15-8:45/mi
- Tempo: ~6:30/mi
- Threshold: 6:15-6:20/mi

Key Principle:
- Easy runs must be truly easy to recover
- Hard runs must be at specific target paces
- No 'moderate' runs that are too hard to recover but too easy to adapt

documents.title: Full-Spectrum Percentage-Based Training System
documents.content:
Full-Spectrum / Percentage-Based Training is a system popularized by Renato Canova and the 'Italian School' of training. It uses a mathematical approach to training that calculates workout paces based on percentages of current race pace, rather than physiological zones like VO2 max or threshold.

The goal is to build a 'ladder of support' around race pace, enabling the athlete to run specific speeds for longer durations. The system applies universally to all distance events from 800m to the marathon.

Core Philosophy: Training is primarily mathematical, not physiological. Workouts are derived from a mathematical calculation of current capabilities. The goal is to solve a specific problem: 'How do I run speed X for distance Y?' The answer is to improve the ability to run fractionally faster than X (speed support) and fractionally slower than X (endurance support). This creates a direct path to race performance without relying on abstract physiological proxies like VO2 max.

The main problem is supporting race-specific workouts. To improve a race-specific workout (e.g., 6 x 1 mile at race pace), you must build support at ±5% of that pace. Training at 105% of race pace (specific speed) and 95% of race pace (specific endurance) directly elevates your ability to sustain 100% of race pace.

documents.title: The Ladder of Support Concept
documents.content:
The Ladder of Support is a central concept in percentage-based training. Every speed supports the speeds immediately adjacent to it.

There is a cascading effect where 110% pace supports 105% pace, which in turn supports 100% pace (race pace). Conversely, 90% pace supports 95% pace. This 'ladder' ensures that improvements in one area mechanically transfer to race-specific capabilities. Gaps in the ladder break this chain of support.

The system is visualized as a pyramid where the base (endurance) must be wider than the peak (speed) to support the race pace in the middle.

Endurance support matters most: Workouts slower than race pace (endurance support) are prioritized over those faster than race pace. Aerobic energy production is dominant in all distance events (800m and up). Empirically, neglecting endurance is a more fatal error than neglecting speed.

The limits of the ladder: Paces slower than 80% of race pace are considered 'general' conditioning rather than specific race preparation. Similarly, paces faster than 115% are too far removed from race pace to provide direct biomechanical or metabolic support. They are useful for general fitness and recovery but do not directly drive specific race performance.

documents.title: Percentage-Based Periodization: General to Specific
documents.content:
In percentage-based training, the order of operations follows the principle of 'training to add, not to replace.' Training phases move from general fitness to 'special' (supportive) work, and finally to specific race simulation.

Phase 1 — General: Focus on fundamental fitness at 70-90% of race pace and running mechanics. This builds the base of the pyramid.

Phase 2 — Race-Supportive (Special): Focus on 90% of race pace (Special Endurance) and 110% of race pace (Special Speed). These paces directly support the race pace zone.

Phase 3 — Race-Specific: Focus on 95%, 100%, and 105% of race pace. This is the sharpening phase where the athlete simulates and extends race-pace performance.

This ensures the athlete has the structural and metabolic capacity to handle the specific intensity of the final phase. Each phase builds on the previous one rather than replacing it.

documents.title: Calculating Training Paces from Current Fitness
documents.content:
In percentage-based training, paces must be calculated based on what you can do today, not what you want to run in the future. Training adapts the body from its current state.

Trying to train at a 'goal pace' that is significantly faster than current ability disconnects the mathematical ladder, causing the workout to target the wrong energy systems or lead to failure and injury. You must bridge the gap gradually.

As fitness improves, 100% of race pace becomes faster. Continuing to train at old paces effectively changes the workout from 'specific' to 'general' (or endurance support). Paces must be updated via race results or fitness assessments to keep the mathematical percentages accurate and the training stimulus effective.

The 5% Rule: Decreasing pace by 5% allows an athlete to sustain that pace for approximately twice the distance. For example, 10K pace is roughly 95% of 5K pace; Half Marathon pace is roughly 95% of 10K pace. This relationship helps calculate training paces across all percentage zones.

Example pace zones relative to 5K race pace:
- 110% = faster than 5K pace (speed support)
- 105% = slightly faster than 5K pace (specific speed)
- 100% = 5K race pace
- 95% = approximately 10K pace (specific endurance)
- 90% = approximately half marathon pace (special endurance)
- 80-85% = general endurance pace

documents.title: Workout Progression: Volume, Extension, and Density
documents.content:
In percentage-based training, you should never repeat the exact same workout — always increase the stimulus. To force adaptation, the stress must increase. This is done via three levers:

1. Volume: Increasing total distance at the target pace. For example, progressing from 5 miles to 6 miles of total work at 95% race pace.

2. Extension: Increasing the length of individual repetitions at the same pace while keeping total volume similar. For example: Week 1: 10 x 600m at 105% pace → Week 3: 8 x 800m at 105% pace → Week 5: 6 x 1000m at 105% pace. Extension is highlighted as a vastly underused tool that increases physiological stimulus without increasing mechanical stress.

3. Density: Decreasing recovery time between repetitions. For example, going from 3 minutes rest to 2 minutes rest between 1K repeats.

Extension Progression is the primary method of improvement — extending the distance one can run at a specific speed. The progression example shows how the total work stays roughly similar (~6000m) but the rep length increases, demanding the athlete sustain the target pace for longer continuous efforts.

All three levers can be combined over a training cycle, but extension should be prioritized as it most directly supports the goal of running a specific pace for a longer distance.

documents.title: Modulation: Balancing Workout Stress and Recovery
documents.content:
Modulation is a key principle in percentage-based training: as workouts become harder and more specific, the recovery time between them must increase. This is called 'increasing modulation.'

A general workout at 80% of race pace might require only 1 day of recovery. But a highly specific, high-volume workout at 100-105% of race pace (like those done by top elites) might require 4-5 days of easy running afterward.

Burnout occurs when athletes increase workout intensity without correspondingly increasing the recovery buffer. The more specific and demanding the training becomes in the final phase of preparation, the more recovery days are needed between quality sessions.

Practical application:
- General phase: Quality sessions every 2-3 days
- Special phase: Quality sessions every 3-4 days
- Specific phase: Quality sessions every 4-5 days (with easy/recovery running between)

The easy days between quality sessions are not wasted days — they allow the adaptations from the hard workout to take hold. Pair greater stress with greater recovery to avoid overtraining and maximize the benefit of each quality session.
